beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The project has models speak as historical figures like Roman engineers and NASA mathematicians. The aim is not extracting answers or optimizing productivity but exploring different conversations. These experts' methods became standard procedure and their excellence became the baseline. A simulated 1955 computer asks whether the canyon remains after the river, method outliving name, is forgotten.

artifactLines: The project has models speak as historical figures like Roman engineers and NASA mathematicians.
The aim is not extracting answers or optimizing productivity but exploring different conversations.
These experts' methods became standard procedure and their excellence became the baseline.
A simulated 1955 computer asks whether the canyon remains after the river, method outliving name, is forgotten.

beats.narration_text: The image of a simulated 1955 computer asking whether 'the canyon remains even after the river is forgotten,' and models speaking as Roman engineers and NASA mathematicians, moved me past the usual answer-extraction view of AI. I want to explore memory as legacy. Help me: (1) explain what it technically means to have a model speak in a historical persona's methods; (2) unpack the canyon-and-river metaphor as a claim about knowledge outliving names; (3) design one conversation that tests whether methods, not just facts, transferred.

props.segment: The Question Nobody Expected to Ask

output: Let me tell you about a trap I set for my students
I told them: spend six weeks having conversations with AI
Here’s what actually happened: they discovered that evaluation it

beats.narration_text: Written by Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. Humanitarians AI is an educational bridge program where Fellows learn experiential AI by doing AI — building real projects and shipping real research.
